selected_sources.title: ICCT UK ETS Maritime Expansion Brief
selected_sources.snippet: # Expanding the UK Emissions Trading Scheme to International Maritime Emissions. The United Kingdom Emissions Trading Scheme (UK ETS) came into effect in 2021 to limit greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from key sectors in support of the UK’s 2050 net-zero target. In 2023, the UK ETS Authority announced it would expand the scheme to include domestic shipping beginning in 2026, covering emissions from vessels of 5,000 gross tonnage (GT) or more when at berth, at anchor, or traveling between UK ports. The 2026 scope covers just 15% of UK-related shipping emissions. The 2026 UK ETS scope — in-port and domestic voyages from ships ≥ 5,000 GT — covers just 2.5 Mt, or 15% of total emissions. The UK’s Emissions Trading Scheme is expanding to domestic maritime shipping in 2026—a welcome step, but one that covers just 15% of UK-related shipping emissions. UK Emissions Trading Scheme: Scope Expansion – Emissions from international maritime voyages consultation.

selected_sources.title: Stephenson Harwood UK ETS Maritime Detail Note
selected_sources.snippet: The newly published details include: + confirmation of a tonnage threshold of 5,000 GT, subject to review in 2028 when it may be lowered to 400 GT; + confirmation there will not be a de minimis threshold for emissions, subject to review in 2028 – this means all emissions falling within scope of the scheme will incur costs; + confirmation of a simplified monitoring approach for ships conducting more than 300 similar domestic voyages per year; + clarification that emissions on voyages between mainland UK and Northern Ireland will be subject to 50% inclusion rather than 100% – this is to achieve consistency of carbon pricing as compared to voyages between mainland UK and the Republic of Ireland; + clarification that emissions from international voyages will be included in the future, potentially from 2028 – this is a particularly important development driven by the prospective linking of the EU ETS and UK ETS and postponement of the adoption of the IMO NZF, although subject to further consultation and negotiations with the EU; + confirmation of exemption for ferry services to Scotland’s islands and to peninsular communities – this is to avoid negative impacts on remote communities and is subject to review in 2028; + confirmation of exemption for fish processing and fish catching vessels – this is to maintain parity with the EU ETS and is subject to review in 2028; + delayed inclusion, from 1 July 2026 to 1 January 2027, of offshore vessels – meaning offshore vessels will fall within the UK ETS and EU ETS for the first time at the same time; ANOTHER EMISSIONS TRADING SCHEME FOR MARITIME - MORE DETAILS ON THE UK ETS 4 December 2025 ANOTHER EMISSIONS TRADING SCHEME FOR MARITIME – MORE DETAILS ON THE UK ETS + delayed surrender, from 30 April 2027 to 30 April 2028, of allowances for emissions in 2026 – this means the first surrender deadline under

selected_sources.title: Lloyd's List UK ETS Domestic Shipping Consultation Report
selected_sources.snippet: This site is operated by a business or businesses owned by Maritime Insights & Intelligence Limited, registered in England and Wales with company number 13831625 and address c/o Hackwood Secretaries Limited, One Silk Street, London EC2Y 8HQ, United Kingdom. # UK to consult on expanding ETS to domestic shipping. The UK Government has launched a second consultation on expanding its ETS to domestic shipping, this time focused on implementation. THE UK Government has launched a consultation on how to implement its plans to expand the UK Emissions Trading Scheme to domestic shipping. rejected_sources.title: UK ETS for maritime: Key takeaways and comparison with the EU ETS
rejected_sources.snippet: From 1 July 2026, the UK will bring domestic maritime emissions into the UK ETS, with a proposal to capture a share of international voyages from 2028, to align more closely with current EU coverage. In the EU, the ETS regulation for shipping has been live since 2024 (the "EU ETS") and captures 100% of emissions on domestic EU/EEA voyages and 50% of emissions on international voyages. From 2027, each compliance period in the UK runs from 1 January to 31 December, with annual reporting by the following 31 March and surrendering of allowances by 30 April, in each case in the year following the relevant compliance period. To compare the EU ETS and UK ETS, from 2026, the EU ETS captures 100% of emissions on domestic EU/EEA voyages and 50% of emissions on international voyages where either the origin or the destination is an EU/EEA port. By contrast, the UK ETS will capture 100% of emissions on domestic UK-to-UK port voyages.

rejected_sources.title: UK ETS 2026: Aviation, Maritime and Sector Expansion Explained
rejected_sources.snippet: # UK ETS 2026: Aviation, Maritime and Sector Expansion Explained. The UK Emissions Trading Scheme, commonly referred to as the UK ETS, is a central pillar of the United Kingdom’s carbon pricing framework. Established following the UK’s departure from the European Union, the scheme replaced the UK’s participation in the EU Emissions Trading System and now operates as a standalone carbon market. As the UK government moves to expand the scheme to additional sectors including maritime transport and waste, and as Phase II planning extends the system beyond 2030, companies both inside and outside the UK must understand how the system works and what compliance will require. For multinational corporations, exporters to the UK, and shipping operators serving UK ports, the UK ETS extends far beyond a domestic climate policy. This article explains what the UK ETS is, which sectors are covered, how the scheme functions, what changes are coming in 2026 and beyond, and what companies need to do now to prepare.

rejected_sources.title: UK ETS for maritime: Key takeaways | Kromann Reumert
rejected_sources.snippet: From 1 July 2026, the UK will bring domestic maritime emissions into the UK ETS, with a proposal to capture a share of international voyages from 2028, to align more closely with current EU coverage. In the EU, the ETS regulation for shipping has been live since 2024 (the "EU ETS") and captures 100% of emissions on domestic EU/EEA voyages and 50% of emissions on international voyages. From 2027, each compliance period in the UK runs from 1 January to 31 December, with annual reporting by the following 31 March and surrendering of allowances by 30 April, in each case in the year following the relevant compliance period. To compare the EU ETS and UK ETS, from 2026, the EU ETS captures 100% of emissions on domestic EU/EEA voyages and 50% of emissions on international voyages where either the origin or the destination is an EU/EEA port. By contrast, the UK ETS will capture 100% of emissions on domestic UK-to-UK port voyages.

rejected_sources.title: Expanding the UK ETS to international maritime
rejected_sources.snippet:
Expanding the UK ETS to international maritime: Emissions coverage and revenue impacts
International Council on Clean Transportation
1990 subscribers
1 likes
29 views
19 May 2026
The UK's Emissions Trading Scheme is expanding to domestic maritime shipping in 2026—a welcome step, but one that covers just 15% of UK-related shipping emissions. 🚢 

New ICCT analysis looks at what it could mean to go further. The numbers make the case for expansion: 

📊 16.4 Mt of CO₂e emitted by UK-related shipping in 2023  

⚠️ 15% of those emissions are covered by the UK ETS  

4️⃣x the current emissions coverage is within reach by expanding to cover international voyages and smaller ships 

💷 £570–£710M in potential annual revenue generated from full expansion 

With the UK government now considering whether to include international voyages originating or ending in UK ports, and a coverage review expected, the window to improve the scheme is open. Our analysis sets out a stepwise plan for what that expansion could look like— and what the benefits of each step are. 

🔗 Read the full brief: https://theicct.org/publication/expanding-the-uk-emissions-trading-scheme-to-international-maritime-emissions-may26/

weighted_sources.title: Expanding the UK Emissions Trading Scheme to International Maritime Emissions - International Council on Clean Transportation
weighted_sources.snippet: # Expanding the UK Emissions Trading Scheme to International Maritime Emissions. The United Kingdom Emissions Trading Scheme (UK ETS) came into effect in 2021 to limit greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from key sectors in support of the UK’s 2050 net-zero target. In 2023, the UK ETS Authority announced it would expand the scheme to include domestic shipping beginning in 2026, covering emissions from vessels of 5,000 gross tonnage (GT) or more when at berth, at anchor, or traveling between UK ports. The 2026 scope covers just 15% of UK-related shipping emissions. The 2026 UK ETS scope — in-port and domestic voyages from ships ≥ 5,000 GT — covers just 2.5 Mt, or 15% of total emissions. The UK’s Emissions Trading Scheme is expanding to domestic maritime shipping in 2026—a welcome step, but one that covers just 15% of UK-related shipping emissions.
